name: Pujol
description: Enrique Olvera's flagship restaurant, consistently ranked among the world's best. The mole madre — aged over 1,000 days — is the centerpiece. Tasting menu only, book well in advance.
address: Tennyson 133, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11550 Ciudad de México

name: Contramar
description: Roma Norte institution serving impeccable Mexican seafood since 1998. The tuna tostadas and red-and-green grilled fish are legendary. Loud, packed, and worth every minute of the wait.
address: Durango 200, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Quintonil
description: Jorge Vallejo's Polanco restaurant — ingredients-first Mexican cooking that feels contemporary without being gimmicky. The avocado tostada and ant larva dishes are extraordinary. Book alongside Pujol.
address: Newton 55, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11560 Ciudad de México

name: Maximo Bistrot
description: Eduardo García's Roma Norte bistro does French technique with Mexican produce better than almost anywhere. The menu changes daily based on the market. Relaxed room, serious food, reasonable prices. Book for dinner.
address: Tonalá 133, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: El Cardenal
description: The definitive traditional Mexican breakfast in the Centro Histórico — hot chocolate, pan dulce, and the finest enchiladas in the city. Three floors of Talavera tiles, mariachis on weekends, and decades of regulars.
address: Palma 23, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, 06000 Ciudad de México

name: Rosetta
description: Elena Reygadas' Roma Norte restaurant in a restored Porfiriato mansion. Italian technique with Mexican ingredients done effortlessly. The bakery next door opens at 8am and is worth the detour alone.
address: Colima 166, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Licorería Limantour
description: Consistently one of the world's 50 best bars and the anchor of Roma Norte's drinking scene. Precise and creative mezcal and tequila cocktails. Come at 6pm to snag a table before the evening crowd arrives.
address: Álvaro Obregón 106, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Zócalo
description: The vast main plaza of Mexico City — one of the largest in the world, anchored by the Metropolitan Cathedral and the National Palace. Diego Rivera's murals inside the palace are free and unmissable. Best early morning before the crowds arrive.
address: Plaza de la Constitución, Centro Histórico, 06060 Ciudad de México

name: Chapultepec Park
description: One of the great urban parks of the Americas — 686 hectares of forest, lakes, and museums in the heart of the city. The hilltop Chapultepec Castle has the best panoramic view in CDMX. Walk the lake path on a Sunday morning when half the city is out.
address: Bosque de Chapultepec, Miguel Hidalgo, 11580 Ciudad de México

name: Palacio de Bellas Artes
description: Mexico City's most spectacular building — art nouveau exterior in white Carrara marble, art deco interior at jaw-dropping scale. Free to enter the lobby. Murals by Rivera, Orozco, and Siqueiros are among the greatest in the world.
address: Av. Juárez S/N, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, 06050 Ciudad de México

name: Xochimilco
description: The ancient floating gardens of the Aztecs — hire a trajinera for a few hours on the canals, bring drinks from the market, flag down the floating food boats, and let the mariachis find you. A Saturday afternoon classic.
address: Xochimilco, Ciudad de México

name: Gran Cantina Filomeno
description: A beloved Roma Norte cantina with the feel of old Mexico City — red booths, cold beer, micheladas, and botanas that arrive without asking. Loud, democratic, and exactly the kind of place you stumble into and stay for three hours.
address: Colima 378, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: MUNAL
description: The Museo Nacional de Arte occupies a magnificent Beaux-Arts palace in the Centro Histórico — two floors of Mexican art from the viceregal period through the 20th century. The equestrian statue of Charles IV out front is one of the best photo spots in the city.
address: Tacuba 8, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, 06010 Ciudad de México

name: Biblioteca Vasconcelos
description: One of the most spectacular libraries in the world — a vast hanging bookshelf structure designed by Alberto Kalach, with a Gabriel Orozco whale skeleton suspended in the atrium. Free entry. Worth a metro ride to Buenavista just to stand inside.
address: Eje 1 Nte. Mosqueta S/N, Buenavista, Cuauhtémoc, 06350 Ciudad de México

name: Caldo
description: A small, focused Roma Norte restaurant known for exceptional broths and slow-cooked Mexican comfort food. Minimal décor, maximum flavour. The kind of neighbourhood spot that earns a cult following entirely by word of mouth.
address: Tonalá 121, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Caliscos Mexicanos
description: A Polanco restaurant serving regional Mexican cuisine from the state of Jalisco with real craft — birria, torta ahogada, and excellent aguas frescas in a handsome dining room. Alejandro Dumas location.
address: Alejandro Dumas 125, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11560 Ciudad de México

name: La Cuadra
description: A no-frills Condesa steakhouse that does one thing and does it right — wood-fired arrachera, excellent guacamole, and cold beer. The lunch crowd of regulars is part of the atmosphere. Don't skip the tortillas.
address: Iztaccihuatl 32, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06140 Ciudad de México

name: Maizajo
description: A Polanco restaurant devoted entirely to the corn plant in all its forms — heirloom varieties prepared as antojitos, tlayudas, memelas, and more. One of the most thoughtful expressions of Mexican culinary identity in the city.
address: Presidente Masaryk 513, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11560 Ciudad de México

name: El Bazar Sábado
description: San Ángel's Saturday artisan market in a 17th-century mansion — high-quality Mexican crafts, jewellery, textiles, and folk art arranged around a central courtyard with a good restaurant inside. Only open Saturdays. A perfect half-morning in the south of the city.
address: Plaza San Jacinto 11, San Ángel, Álvaro Obregón, 01000 Ciudad de México

name: Taco Tasting Room
description: A focused, modern taco concept in Roma Norte where seasonal ingredients and traditional technique produce tacos that are taken seriously. Small plates, rotating menu, and a mezcal list to match. The antithesis of a tourist taco joint.
address: Orizaba 101, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Sud 777
description: Edgar Núñez's Jardines del Pedregal restaurant is one of the most exciting kitchens in Mexico — contemporary Mexican cooking in a stunning garden setting, with a tasting menu that changes with the seasons. Worth the taxi south.
address: Blvd. de la Luz 777, Jardines del Pedregal, Álvaro Obregón, 01900 Ciudad de México

name: Ardente
description: Neapolitan-style wood-fired pizza in Roma Norte made by people who care deeply about dough. The margherita is the benchmark and it's excellent. A rare thing in Mexico City: a pizza worth choosing over tacos.
address: Orizaba 96, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Tetetlán
description: A Condesa restaurant bringing serious Oaxacan cooking to the city — tlayudas, mole negro, mezcal from small producers, and the best memelas you'll find outside of Oaxaca itself. Warm, noisy, and genuinely excellent.
address: Tamaulipas 120, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06140 Ciudad de México

name: Casa de la Bola
description: A hidden jewel in Tacubaya — a perfectly preserved 18th-century hacienda filled with Mexican antiques, colonial furniture, and Baroque paintings collected over decades. One of the most atmospheric houses in the city, virtually unknown to tourists.
address: Parque Lira 136, Tacubaya, Miguel Hidalgo, 11850 Ciudad de México

name: Santo Spirito
description: A Roma Norte trattoria doing Italian cooking with Mexican ingredients and none of the pretension. Handmade pasta, natural wines, and a room full of locals who come back every week. The kind of neighbourhood restaurant every city needs more of.
address: Colima 247, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Museo de Arte Moderno
description: Mexico's great modern art museum inside Chapultepec Park — Tamayo, Siqueiros, Kahlo's famous 'The Two Fridas', and a sculpture garden you can wander freely. The circular 1960s building is itself worth seeing.
address: Av. Gandhi S/N, Bosque de Chapultepec, Miguel Hidalgo, 11580 Ciudad de México

name: Café Nin
description: A well-designed café-bistro in the Juárez neighbourhood doing excellent coffee, pastries, and a short all-day menu. The room is calm, the light is good, and the espresso tonic is one of the better ones in the city.
address: Havre 73, Juárez, Cuauhtémoc, 06600 Ciudad de México

name: Café Trucha
description: A tiny Roma Norte coffee shop with serious single-origin sourcing and no-nonsense preparation. The kind of place where the barista knows the farm by name. Small, often crowded at 9am, and completely worth it.
address: Zacatecas 115, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Delirio
description: Roma Norte's beloved deli-café — coffee, wine, excellent sandwiches, and a small but carefully chosen grocery that supplies half the neighbourhood's dinner parties. Monica Patiño's place feels like the living room of Roma Norte.
address: Monterrey 116, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Testarudo
description: A natural wine bar in Roma Norte that arrived before the natural wine wave and has been pouring interesting bottles to the neighbourhood ever since. Small plates, zero pretension, and a list that actually rewards curiosity.
address: Álvaro Obregón 64, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Maque Café
description: A Condesa café with good coffee, a shaded terrace, and a rotating selection of Mexican pastries and light plates. The kind of place you find by walking slowly through the neighbourhood and then come back to every morning.
address: Atlixco 73, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06140 Ciudad de México

name: La Gruta
description: A restaurant inside a natural cave at the foot of the Teotihuacán archaeological zone — eat lunch inside the lit cavern after touring the pyramids. Touristic but genuinely atmospheric. The ideal way to end a pyramid morning.
address: Zona Arqueológica de Teotihuacan, San Juan Teotihuacán, 55800 Estado de México

name: Los Dinamos
description: A forested canyon park in Magdalena Contreras at the edge of the city — hiking trails, a river, picnic areas, and weekend gordita stands that make it feel like a different country. One of the best easy escapes from the urban core.
address: Parque Nacional Los Dinamos, Magdalena Contreras, 10710 Ciudad de México

name: Nido de Quetzalcóatl
description: Javier Senosiain's organic architecture masterpiece in Naucalpan — a house shaped like a snake/flower that emerges from the hillside, covered in mosaic and surrounded by sculpture. One of the most extraordinary private residences in the world. Day tours available.
address: Pico de Orizaba 5, Naucalpan, Estado de México

name: Los Amantes
description: A mezcalería in the Centro Histórico with hundreds of expressions from small Oaxacan and Guerrero producers — the kind of list you need a guide to navigate, and the bartenders are exactly that. Unpretentious and deeply educational.
address: Venustiano Carranza 49, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, 06060 Ciudad de México

name: San Juan Bautista Monastery
description: A 16th-century Dominican monastery and church facing Coyoacán's main plaza — one of the oldest in Mexico City, with a serene cloister and an ornate colonial interior. The attached garden café is the best spot for a coffee after visiting the Blue House.
address: Plaza Hidalgo 1, Coyoacán, 04000 Ciudad de México

name: Capilla de la Conchita
description: The oldest church in Mexico City — a tiny 1521 chapel on a quiet Coyoacán plaza that has barely changed in five centuries. The simple stone facade hides an extraordinarily intimate interior. Easy to miss, impossible to forget once found.
address: Higuera S/N, Coyoacán, 04000 Ciudad de México

name: Capilla de Santa Catarina de Siena
description: A 16th-century chapel on one of Coyoacán's quietest plazas — the parroquia that served the neighbourhood before the larger San Juan Bautista was built. The surrounding streets have the most authentic village atmosphere in the borough.
address: Plaza Santa Catarina, Coyoacán, 04000 Ciudad de México

name: Expendio de Maiz Sin Nombre
description: No menu, no sign, no reservations — a small Colonia Roma spot serving a rotating series of masa-based dishes made from heirloom corn varieties, sourced daily. One of the most interesting and uncompromising eating experiences in Mexico City.
address: Campos Elíseos, Colonia Roma Sur, Cuauhtémoc, 06760 Ciudad de México

name: Panadería Rosetta
description: Elena Reygadas' bakery adjacent to Rosetta is one of the best in Latin America — guava and cream cheese rolls, excellent coffee, sourdough, and morning pastries made with Mexican grains. A Roma Norte essential from 8am.
address: Colima 179, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Odette
description: A Polanco restaurant doing French bistro classics with impeccable sourcing — steak tartare, duck confit, a serious wine list. The room is comfortable and unhurried in a way that's rare in this part of the city. Perfect for a long lunch.
address: Emilio Castelar 149, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11560 Ciudad de México

name: Salón Palomilla
description: A Roma Norte cantina that got gentrified but kept its soul — formica tables, corridos on the sound system, and micheladas that arrive without ice. The afternoon crowd is exactly the mix the neighbourhood is known for.
address: Sonora 68, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Taverna
description: A Roma Norte wine bar with a well-chosen list of natural and low-intervention bottles from Mexico and Europe. Food comes from the kitchen at Rosetta. Intimate space, long evenings, and the kind of crowd that knows what it's drinking.
address: Colima 140, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Pistilo
description: A verdant cocktail bar in Roma Norte with plants covering every surface and a menu of botanical-forward drinks made with Mexican spirits. The perfect midday retreat — cool, green, and unhurried in the Roma afternoon heat.
address: Orizaba 160, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: La Once Mil
description: A Condesa mezcal and cocktail bar that opens late and earns its place as a neighbourhood anchor. The mezcal selection covers small producers across six states and the bartenders pour with genuine enthusiasm.
address: Tamaulipas 88, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06140 Ciudad de México

name: Jacinta
description: A hotel restaurant in Polanco that transcends its setting — the kitchen produces elegant modern Mexican food using ingredients sourced directly from small producers. The chile en nogada (seasonal) is among the best in the city.
address: Campos Elíseos 204, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11560 Ciudad de México

name: Fonda Fina
description: Juan Cabrera's Roma Norte homage to the Mexican fonda — traditional dishes executed with precision and respect. The best arroz con leche in the city, chiles rellenos to make you reconsider the dish, and a warm room that earns its full tables.
address: Medellín 79, Roma Norte, Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México

name: Siembra Tortillería
description: A Condesa tortillería and taquería devoted to heirloom corn varieties — watch the masa being ground and pressed, then eat the results immediately. The simplest and most fundamental Mexican food done with extraordinary care.
address: Ámsterdam 135, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06100 Ciudad de México

name: Nicos
description: The great traditional Mexican restaurant of the city's north — open since 1957, now run by Gerardo Vázquez Lugo who has earned a Michelin recommendation and every award without changing the soul of the place. The mole and the chiles en nogada are benchmarks.
address: Av. Cuitláhuac 3102, Clavería, Azcapotzalco, 02080 Ciudad de México

name: Amaya
description: A Condesa cocktail bar and small plates restaurant that hits the perfect midpoint between relaxed and excellent. The drinks are creative without being exhausting, the food is good enough to make a full evening of it.
address: Francisco Márquez 105, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06140 Ciudad de México

name: Ticuchi
description: Enrique Olvera's mezcalería in Polanco — a small, focused bar dedicated entirely to agave spirits from small producers. The list is one of the most serious in the city and the staff know every bottle on it.
address: Tennyson 117, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, 11550 Ciudad de México

name: La Fonda Margarita
description: A Colonia del Valle breakfast institution open only mornings until they run out — caldos, guisados, and fresh tortillas served from large clay pots. Cash only, plastic tables, no menu, and a queue of regulars every day from 7am. A Mexico City pilgrimage.
address: Adolfo Prieto 1364, Colonia del Valle, Benito Juárez, 03100 Ciudad de México

name: Tacos El Güero
description: The taco stand that anchors Coyoacán market — canasta tacos stuffed with chicharrón, beans, and potato served at a folding table inside the covered market. Arrive before noon. Absolute perfection for under 50 pesos.
address: Mercado de Coyoacán, Ignacio Allende S/N, Coyoacán, 04000 Ciudad de México

name: Saint
description: A Condesa bar that does exactly what a neighbourhood bar should — good drinks, comfortable seating, no attitude, and a playlist that improves as the evening progresses. A reliable anchor for a night that starts here and goes elsewhere.
address: Ámsterdam 333, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06100 Ciudad de México

name: Hugo
description: A Condesa restaurant doing contemporary Mexican cooking with a market-driven menu and a particular talent for vegetables. The mole blanco and the rotating raw bar are consistent highlights. Casual enough for lunch, good enough for a special dinner.
address: Tamaulipas 30, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06140 Ciudad de México

name: El Hidalguense
description: The best barbacoa in Mexico City — slow-cooked lamb in maguey leaves overnight, served from massive clay pots on weekends only. The consommé alone is worth the trip to Colonia Doctores. Cash only, open Saturday and Sunday until they run out.
address: Campeche 155, Colonia Doctores, Cuauhtémoc, 06720 Ciudad de México

name: Mi Compa Chava
description: A Condesa taquería doing birria de res — braised beef tacos served with a rich consommé for dipping, loaded with cilantro, onion, and salsa verde. A Mexico City obsession done properly. The queue moves fast and the payoff is immediate.
address: Mazatlán 130, Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, 06100 Ciudad de México

name: Museo Nacional de Antropología
description: One of the great museums on earth — a vast collection of pre-Columbian artefacts spanning every major Mesoamerican civilisation: Aztec, Maya, Olmec, Toltec, Zapotec. The Aztec Sun Stone alone justifies the visit. Plan a full day; the building itself, designed by Pedro Ramírez Vázquez around a central courtyard and cascade, is an architectural landmark.
address: Av. Paseo de la Reforma s/n, Chapultepec, Miguel Hidalgo, CDMX

name: Museo Frida Kahlo (La Casa Azul)
description: The cobalt-blue house in Coyoacán where Frida Kahlo was born, lived, and died — now a museum preserving her studio, personal belongings, clothing, and the wheelchair at which she painted in her final years. One of the most intimate and emotionally charged artist's houses anywhere. Book tickets well in advance; they sell out weeks ahead.
address: Londres 247, Del Carmen, Coyoacán, CDMX

name: Museo Soumaya
description: Carlos Slim's shimmering aluminium-clad museum at Plaza Carso in Polanco — 66,000 objects spanning 3,000 years, including the largest collection of Rodin sculptures outside France and an extraordinary collection of colonial and pre-Columbian art. Free entry. The building's undulating exterior is one of Mexico City's most striking pieces of contemporary architecture.
address: Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra 303, Granada, Miguel Hidalgo, CDMX

name: Museo Tamayo
description: Rufino Tamayo's personal collection donated to the Mexican people — international modern and contemporary art housed in a beautiful brutalist building in Chapultepec. Rotating exhibitions of global contemporary art alongside the permanent Tamayo collection. Less visited than the Anthropology Museum next door; consequently more peaceful.
address: Paseo de la Reforma 51, Bosque de Chapultepec, Miguel Hidalgo, CDMX

name: Museo del Templo Mayor
description: The ruins of the great Aztec temple of Tenochtitlán, discovered accidentally in 1978 when workers were laying electrical cable — exposed in the heart of the historic centre. The adjacent museum houses the extraordinary finds: the Coyolxauhqui stone, ritual offerings, and thousands of artefacts that reveal the sophistication of the Aztec capital. Walk out and you're back in the Zócalo.
address: Seminario 8, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: Castillo de Chapultepec
description: The only royal castle in North America — a hilltop palace above Chapultepec Park that served as the official residence of Emperor Maximilian and later Mexican presidents. Now the Museo Nacional de Historia, with murals by Siqueiros and O'Gorman, Maximilian and Carlota's private apartments, and sweeping views over the city from the terraces.
address: Bosque de Chapultepec I Sección, Miguel Hidalgo, CDMX

name: Museo Dolores Olmedo
description: A 16th-century hacienda in La Noria, near Xochimilco, housing the largest private collection of Diego Rivera and Frida Kahlo works in the world — amassed by Rivera's patron and companion Dolores Olmedo. The grounds are filled with Mexican hairless xoloitzcuintli dogs and peacocks wandering freely between the artwork. One of the most unusual and rewarding museums in Mexico City.
address: Av. México 5843, La Noria, Xochimilco, CDMX

name: Museo Jumex
description: Mexico's foremost contemporary art museum at Plaza Carso, next to the Soumaya — a David Chipperfield-designed building housing the Jumex Foundation's world-class collection of international contemporary art. Smaller and more curated than the Soumaya, with a strong programme of temporary exhibitions and one of the best art bookshops in the city.
address: Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra 303, Granada, Miguel Hidalgo, CDMX

name: Palacio Nacional
description: The seat of the Mexican federal government on the Zócalo, housing Diego Rivera's monumental mural series depicting the entire history of Mexico — from pre-Columbian civilisation through the Revolution. Free entry. The murals wrap around the main staircase and are among the greatest works of public art in the Americas. Arrive early; lines form later in the day.
address: Plaza de la Constitución s/n, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: La Merced
description: The largest traditional market in Mexico City — a sprawling, overwhelming complex in the eastern historic centre covering dozens of blocks and several buildings, each dedicated to different products: dried chiles, spices, herbs, sweets, piñatas, meat, seafood. Raw, loud, and completely authentic. Not a tourist market; a working market that feeds the city. Go in the morning.
address: Anillo de Circunvalación, Merced Balbuena, Venustiano Carranza, CDMX

name: Mercado de Jamaica
description: Mexico City's wholesale flower market — open 24 hours, spectacular at 3–4am when the trucks arrive from the countryside. Towers of marigolds for Día de los Muertos, hundreds of rose varieties, tropical flowers, and plants at a fraction of retail prices. One of the great sensory experiences in the city. Bring cash.
address: Guillermo Prieto 45, Jamaica, Venustiano Carranza, CDMX

name: Mercado de Medellín
description: The best neighbourhood market in the city — a covered market in Roma Sur with excellent produce, seafood, a strong Cuban food section (legacies of the neighbourhood's immigrant history), and some of the best tacos and tortas you'll find. Less chaotic than La Merced, more real than the tourist markets. Go for breakfast or lunch.
address: Campeche 101, Roma Sur, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: Torre Latinoamericana
description: Mexico City's most beloved skyscraper — a 1956 Art Deco tower that was the tallest building in Latin America for decades. The observation deck on the 44th floor gives the best 360° view of the city basin, the volcanoes, and the vast urban sprawl. On clear days you can see Popocatépetl smoking to the southeast.
address: Eje Central Lázaro Cárdenas 2, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: Palacio de Correos
description: Mexico City's central post office, opened in 1907 — a dazzling Venetian Gothic building by Italian architect Adamo Boari, with ornate ironwork, Moorish arches, and a grand interior that has barely changed since the Porfiriato. Still a functioning post office. Walk in, look up, and marvel. Free to enter.
address: Eje Central Lázaro Cárdenas 1, Centro Histórico, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: Casa Luis Barragán
description: The UNESCO World Heritage home and studio of Luis Barragán — Mexico's greatest architect and one of the most important figures in 20th-century architecture. The house is a masterwork of light, colour, and silence: hot pink walls, volcanic stone floors, and rooms where natural light does everything. Tours must be booked in advance and are strictly limited. Worth every effort.
address: Gral. Francisco Ramírez 12, Ampliación Daniel Garza, Miguel Hidalgo, CDMX

name: Estadio Azteca
description: The most storied football stadium in the world — 87,000 capacity, where Pelé lifted the 1970 World Cup and Maradona scored both the Hand of God and the Goal of the Century in 1986. Still the home ground of Club América and the Mexican national team. Tours available on non-match days; the atmosphere on match nights is extraordinary.
address: Calzada de Tlalpan 3465, Santa Úrsula Coapa, Coyoacán, CDMX

name: Plaza Garibaldi
description: The spiritual home of mariachi music — a square in the historic centre where dozens of mariachi bands in full costume gather every night from around 9pm, available for hire by the song or the hour. Chaotic, loud, and uniquely Mexican. The surrounding pulquerías serve the ancient agave drink in flavoured varieties. Best experienced after midnight when the city's restaurants have emptied out.
address: Plaza Garibaldi, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: Tlatelolco & Plaza de las Tres Culturas
description: Where three civilisations literally occupy the same ground: Aztec ruins of the great market of Tlatelolco, a 16th-century Spanish colonial church built with stones from the demolished pyramid, and a 1960s modernist housing complex. Also the site of the 1968 student massacre, days before the Mexico City Olympics. A profound and layered place. Free entry.
address: Ricardo Flores Magón 1, Nonoalco Tlatelolco, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: Mercado de Artesanías La Ciudadela
description: The best crafts market in Mexico City — a large covered market near the Alameda Central with over 300 stalls representing artisans from every Mexican state. Oaxacan textiles, Talavera ceramics, alebrijes from Oaxaca, silver from Taxco, lacquerware from Michoacán. Prices are fair and negotiation is expected. Far better quality and selection than the tourist stalls in the Zócalo.
address: Plaza de la Ciudadela 1, Centro, Cuauhtémoc, CDMX

name: Bosque de Tlalpan
description: A wild forest reserve in the south of the city — 700 hectares of pine and oak at 2,400 metres, far less manicured than Chapultepec and almost entirely local. Running trails, cycling paths, and picnic areas under a forest canopy that makes you forget you're in a megalopolis. Go on a Sunday morning when the city's serious runners come out.
address: Bosque de Tlalpan, Tlalpan, CDMX

name: Viveros de Coyoacán
description: A working tree nursery turned beloved public park in Coyoacán — shaded pathways through eucalyptus, ash, and pine planted to reforest the city, now used by neighbourhood runners, dog walkers, and anyone who wants to breathe in the south of the city. Quiet, green, and without the crowds of Chapultepec. The Sunday morning running scene here is a Mexico City institution.
address: Madrid s/n, Copilco El Bajo, Coyoacán, CDMX

name: Parque Lincoln
description: Polanco's neighbourhood park and Sunday morning gathering point — a shaded green square with a small zoo, an open-air theatre, and the weekly Mercado del Parque on weekends, which draws the neighbourhood's best food vendors. A good orientation point for Polanco and the base for a morning walk through the area's art deco residential streets.
address: Av. Emilio Castelar s/n, Polanco, Miguel Hidalgo, CDMX

name: Teotihuacán
description: One of the great archaeological sites on earth — a pre-Aztec city of over 100,000 people at its peak, 50km northeast of Mexico City. The Pyramid of the Sun is the third largest pyramid in the world; the Avenue of the Dead stretches 2.5km between ceremonial platforms. Go on a weekday, arrive at opening (8am), and climb the pyramids before the midday heat. Two hours by bus from the Terminal Norte.
address: San Juan Teotihuacán, Estado de México

name: Tepoztlán
description: A magical pueblo mágico 80km south of Mexico City, set in a valley beneath dramatic volcanic cliffs and a small Aztec pyramid on the ridge. Sunday market fills the cobblestone streets with food, crafts, and the best tlayudas outside Oaxaca. The hike up to the Tepozteco pyramid is steep but short. A favourite weekend escape for chilangos — go on a Saturday and stay the night.
address: Tepoztlán, Morelos

name: Taxco
description: Mexico's silver city — a spectacular colonial town in the mountains of Guerrero, 2.5 hours south of Mexico City, where cobblestone streets cascade down the hillside between whitewashed buildings and one of Mexico's finest baroque churches, Santa Prisca. Every shop sells silver: rings, earrings, cutlery, candlesticks. The views from the cable car over the town and valleys are exceptional.
address: Taxco de Alarcón, Guerrero

name: Puebla
description: One of Mexico's finest colonial cities, 2 hours east of Mexico City by highway — a UNESCO World Heritage centre of talavera-tiled buildings, baroque churches, and the birthplace of mole poblano and chiles en nogada. The Zócalo, the Barrio del Artista, and the ex-convent of Santa Catalina are the highlights. The food alone justifies the trip: get to a mole restaurant and a cemitas stall.
address: Puebla de Zaragoza, Puebla
